blocks.text: TypeScript is a strongly typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript, giving you the power of static type checking while keeping full compatibility with every JavaScript runtime.
What is TypeScript?

TypeScript was created by Microsoft in 2012 and has grown into one of the most popular programming languages in the world. It adds optional static typing, interfaces, generics, and modern language features on top of JavaScript, enabling developers to catch bugs at compile time rather than at runtime.
Every valid JavaScript file is also a valid TypeScript file. You can adopt TypeScript gradually — start with .ts extensions and add types incrementally without rewriting existing code.
Why Use TypeScript?

items: Catch type errors at compile time, before the code runs
Improved IDE support with autocompletion, refactoring, and inline documentation
Easier to maintain large codebases with explicit contracts between modules
Supports modern ECMAScript features and compiles down to older JavaScript versions
First-class support in Angular, React, Vue, Node.js, and most major frameworks
Rich ecosystem with type definitions available for thousands of libraries via DefinitelyTyped

blocks.text: TypeScript vs JavaScript
Your First TypeScript Program

blocks.title: Hello TypeScript

blocks.text: The : string annotations after the parameter and return type tell the TypeScript compiler what types are expected. If you pass a number instead of a string, TypeScript will show a compile-time error before the code ever runs.

TypeScript code is never executed directly. The TypeScript compiler (tsc) transforms .ts files into plain .js files, stripping all type annotations in the process. The output JavaScript runs in any browser or Node.js environment.
Setting up TypeScript takes only a few minutes — install the compiler via npm, create a tsconfig.json to configure the project, and start writing typed code immediately.
Installing TypeScript
TypeScript is distributed as an npm package. You can install it globally to use the tsc command anywhere, or locally as a development dependency in a project.

blocks.title: Install TypeScript globally
blocks.code:
# Install globally
npm install -g typescript

# Verify installation
tsc --version
# Output: Version 5.x.x

# Install locally in a project
npm install --save-dev typescript

blocks.text: Compiling TypeScript
Use the tsc command to compile a TypeScript file. Running tsc hello.ts produces a hello.js file in the same directory. You can also use tsc --watch to recompile automatically whenever a file changes.

blocks.title: Compile a single file
blocks.code:
# Compile once
tsc hello.ts

# Compile and watch for changes
tsc hello.ts --watch

# Compile entire project (reads tsconfig.json)
tsc

blocks.text: The tsconfig.json File

The tsconfig.json file is the central configuration for a TypeScript project. It specifies the compiler options, the root files to include, and which files to exclude. Generate a default config with tsc --init.

blocks.title: Typical tsconfig.json
blocks.code:
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "strict": true,
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "rootDir": "./src",
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist"]
}

blocks.text: Key Compiler Options

rows: ECMAScript version for the output JS
Output directory for compiled JS files
Root directory of source TypeScript files
Error when a type is inferred as `any`
Prevents null/undefined from being assigned to other types
Allows default imports from CommonJS modules

blocks.text: Always enable "strict": true in new projects. It activates noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, strictFunctionTypes, and several other checks that catch the most common bugs.
For a Node.js project you also need the type definitions for Node built-ins: run npm install --save-dev @types/node. For a browser-only project these are not needed.
TypeScript provides a rich set of primitive and structural types that let you describe exactly what kind of data a variable, parameter, or return value should hold.
Primitive Types
The three most common primitive types in TypeScript mirror JavaScript primitives: string, number, and boolean. TypeScript also adds bigint and symbol for specialised use cases.

blocks.title: Declaring primitives

blocks.text: Arrays and Tuples
Arrays are typed using either Type[] syntax or the generic form Array<Type>. Both forms are equivalent. Tuples are fixed-length arrays where each position has a specific type.

blocks.title: Arrays

blocks.text: Special Types: any, unknown, never, void

rows: Opt out of type checking (avoid if possible)
Value of unknown type — must narrow before use
Functions that never return (throw or infinite loop)
Functions that return no meaningful value

blocks.title: Special types in practice

blocks.text: null and undefined

With strictNullChecks enabled, null and undefined are their own types and cannot be assigned to other types without an explicit union. This eliminates the most common source of runtime errors in JavaScript.

blocks.title: null and undefined

blocks.text: Avoid using any in production code. If you receive data from an external API, prefer unknown and validate/narrow the type before use. This preserves type safety while still handling truly dynamic data.
TypeScript can automatically infer the type of a variable from its initial value, meaning you do not always need to write explicit type annotations — the compiler figures it out for you.
How Inference Works

When you declare a variable and assign a value in the same statement, TypeScript infers the type from that value. The variable is then treated exactly as if you had written the annotation explicitly — assigning a different type later will produce an error.

blocks.title: Basic inference

blocks.text: Return Type Inference
TypeScript infers function return types from the return statement. If a function always returns a number, TypeScript records the return type as number without any annotation.

blocks.title: Return type inference

blocks.text: Contextual Typing

TypeScript uses contextual typing when the type of an expression is determined by its position in the code. For example, event handler callbacks automatically receive the correct event type based on the listener method they are passed to.

blocks.title: Contextual typing with event listeners

blocks.text: Best Common Type
When TypeScript infers the type of an array literal with mixed values, it computes the best common type — a union of all the value types present.

blocks.title: Best common type inference

blocks.text: When to Write Explicit Annotations

items: When declaring a variable without an initial value: let name: string;
When a function's parameter types cannot be inferred from context
When you want to widen or narrow the inferred type deliberately
When the inferred type is too broad (e.g., inferred as any from JSON.parse)
When documenting public API function return types for readability

blocks.text: Relying on inference for local variables and explicit annotations for function signatures is the most common TypeScript style. It reduces noise while keeping public APIs clearly documented.
Interfaces define the shape of an object — the names and types of its properties and methods — acting as a contract that any conforming object must satisfy.
Defining an Interface
Use the interface keyword to declare an interface. Any object assigned to a variable of that interface type must provide all required properties with the correct types.

blocks.title: Basic interface

blocks.text: Optional and Readonly Properties
Mark a property as optional with ? and as read-only with readonly. Optional properties may be absent; readonly properties cannot be reassigned after initialisation.

blocks.title: Optional and readonly

blocks.text: Method Signatures
Interfaces can describe methods as well as properties. You can write method signatures using either shorthand syntax or as properties with function types.

blocks.title: Interface with methods

blocks.text: Interface Extension
Interfaces can extend one or more other interfaces using the extends keyword, combining their properties into a single type.

blocks.title: Extending interfaces

blocks.text: Declaration Merging

TypeScript supports declaration merging for interfaces: if you declare two interfaces with the same name, TypeScript merges them into a single interface containing all the members. This is especially useful when augmenting third-party library types.

blocks.title: Declaration merging

blocks.text: Prefer interfaces over type aliases for object shapes when working in teams or publishing libraries. Declaration merging makes interfaces more extensible, and they produce clearer error messages in most editors.
Type aliases let you assign a name to any type — from a simple primitive union to a complex object shape — making code more readable and reducing repetition.
Creating a Type Alias
Use the type keyword to create an alias. Unlike interfaces, type aliases can describe not just objects but also primitives, unions, tuples, and function signatures.

blocks.title: Basic type aliases

blocks.text: Function Type Aliases
A type alias can describe a function signature, making it easy to pass callbacks with consistent types throughout a codebase.

blocks.title: Function type alias

blocks.text: Type Aliases vs Interfaces
Recursive Type Aliases
Type aliases can reference themselves to model recursive data structures such as trees or nested JSON.

blocks.title: Recursive type alias

blocks.text: Mapped and Utility Types with Aliases
Type aliases work seamlessly with TypeScript's built-in utility types like Partial, Required, Readonly, and Pick to create derived types without repetition.

blocks.title: Utility types with aliases

blocks.text: Use type for unions, tuples, and derived/utility types. Use interface for object shapes that may need to be extended or merged. Both are equally valid for plain object descriptions.
Union types allow a value to be one of several types, while intersection types combine multiple types into one — together they form the foundation of TypeScript's composable type system.
Union Types
A union type is written with the pipe operator | between two or more types. A value of a union type may be any one of the listed types at a given time.

blocks.title: Union type basics

blocks.text: Discriminated Unions
A discriminated union is a union of object types that each have a common literal property (the discriminant). TypeScript uses that property to narrow the type in switch statements and if-blocks.

blocks.title: Discriminated union pattern

blocks.text: Intersection Types

An intersection type is written with the & operator and creates a type that has all the properties of the combined types. It is often used to merge interfaces or add extra properties to an existing type.

blocks.title: Intersection type

blocks.text: Type Narrowing with Union Types

TypeScript uses several forms of type narrowing to determine which member of a union is active at a given point. The most common narrowing techniques are typeof, instanceof, in checks, and discriminant property checks.

blocks.title: Type narrowing techniques

blocks.text: Union vs Intersection — Summary

rows: Value is either A or B
Value is both A and B
Only those in both A and B
All properties from A and B

blocks.text: When intersecting two types that have a property with the same name but incompatible types (e.g., { id: string } & { id: number }), the resulting type for that property becomes never, making it impossible to satisfy.
TypeScript enhances JavaScript functions with explicit parameter types, return type annotations, optional and default parameters, rest parameters, and function overloads.
Parameter and Return Types
Every function parameter should have a type annotation. The return type is written after the parameter list, preceded by :. If you don't annotate the return type, TypeScript infers it.

blocks.title: Basic function annotations

blocks.text: Optional and Default Parameters
Mark a parameter optional with ?. Its type becomes T | undefined inside the function. Default parameters provide a fallback value and are implicitly optional.

blocks.title: Optional and default parameters

blocks.text: Rest Parameters
A rest parameter collects all remaining arguments into a typed array. It must be the last parameter and is annotated as ...paramName: Type[].

blocks.title: Rest parameters

blocks.text: Function Overloads

Function overloads allow a single function to have multiple call signatures for different combinations of argument types. You declare the overload signatures first, then provide a single implementation signature compatible with all of them.

blocks.title: Function overloads

blocks.text: The this Parameter
TypeScript allows you to declare the type of this as a fake first parameter. This helps when methods are detached from their object and called in a different context.

blocks.title: Typing this in a function

blocks.text: Prefer arrow functions for callbacks inside class methods. Arrow functions capture the outer this lexically, avoiding the common bug where this is undefined in an event handler.
TypeScript classes build on JavaScript ES6 classes by adding access modifiers, parameter properties, abstract classes, and structural compatibility — enabling full object-oriented design patterns.
Defining a Class
TypeScript requires that class properties be declared with their types. Fields can be initialized in the declaration or in the constructor.

blocks.title: Basic class

blocks.text: Access Modifiers
TypeScript provides three access modifiers: public (default, accessible everywhere), private (accessible only within the class), and protected (accessible within the class and its subclasses).

blocks.title: Access modifiers

blocks.text: Parameter Properties
TypeScript shorthand: adding a modifier to a constructor parameter automatically declares a class property and assigns the argument to it — eliminating the need to write this.prop = prop manually.

blocks.title: Parameter properties shorthand

blocks.text: Inheritance and Abstract Classes
Classes can extend one another with extends. Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly; they define a contract that subclasses must implement using the abstract keyword.

blocks.title: Abstract class and inheritance

blocks.text: TypeScript's private is a compile-time check only. At runtime the property is still accessible. For true runtime privacy, use the ECMAScript private field syntax: #balance.
Generics allow you to write reusable functions, classes, and interfaces that work with any type while still enforcing type safety — you get flexibility without sacrificing correctness.
Why Generics?
Without generics you either use any (losing type safety) or write duplicate code for every type. Generics let you write a single definition that adapts to whatever type is provided at the call site.

blocks.title: Generic function

blocks.text: Generic Interfaces and Types
Generics work with interfaces and type aliases. The type parameter is declared in angle brackets after the name: interface Box<T>.

blocks.title: Generic interface

blocks.text: Generic Constraints
Use extends to constrain a type parameter to types that have at least certain properties. This lets you access properties on the generic type safely.

blocks.title: Generic constraints

blocks.text: Generic Classes
A generic class maintains type safety across all its methods. The type parameter is declared on the class and can be used in properties, method parameters, and return types.

blocks.title: Generic stack class

blocks.text: Multiple Type Parameters

blocks.title: Multiple type parameters

blocks.text: By convention, single-letter names like T, K, V, and E are used for type parameters, but you can use any descriptive name such as TItem or TResponse for clarity in complex generics.
Enums define a set of named constants, making code more readable and less error-prone than raw strings or magic numbers — TypeScript supports both numeric and string enums.
Numeric Enums
By default, enum members are assigned incrementing numeric values starting at 0. You can override the starting value or assign values to individual members.

blocks.title: Numeric enum

blocks.text: String Enums
String enums provide more readable values in debugging and serialisation. Each member must be explicitly initialised with a string literal. String enums do not have a reverse mapping.

blocks.title: String enum

blocks.text: Const Enums
Prefix an enum with const to tell the compiler to inline the enum values at every usage site. This produces smaller and faster JavaScript output because no enum object is emitted.

blocks.title: Const enum

blocks.text: Enums vs Union Types

blocks.title: Enum in a switch statement

blocks.text: For most use cases in modern TypeScript, a union of string literals (e.g., type Direction = 'up' | 'down' | 'left' | 'right') is simpler and produces leaner JavaScript than a full enum. Use enums when you need the runtime object or reverse mapping.
Arrays hold a variable number of elements of the same (or union) type, while tuples represent fixed-length arrays where each position has a distinct, known type.
Typed Arrays
TypeScript array types are written as Type[] or Array<Type>. You can also use readonly arrays to prevent mutation.

blocks.title: Array declarations

blocks.text: Defining Tuples
A tuple type is an array type with a fixed number of elements where the type of each position is specified. Accessing an index outside the defined range produces a compile-time error.

blocks.title: Tuple basics

blocks.text: Named Tuple Elements
TypeScript 4.0+ supports named tuple elements for better documentation. The names are purely for readability — they do not affect the runtime structure.

blocks.title: Named tuple elements

blocks.text: Optional and Rest Elements in Tuples
Tuple elements can be marked optional with ?, and a rest element can capture a variable-length tail of the tuple.

blocks.title: Optional and rest in tuples

blocks.text: Common Array Methods with Types

blocks.title: Typed array operations

blocks.text: Use tuples when a function needs to return multiple values of different types — it is a cleaner alternative to an object in simple cases. For example, React's useState hook returns a [state, setter] tuple.
Type assertions let you tell the TypeScript compiler to treat a value as a specific type when you have information that the compiler does not — use them carefully, as they bypass type checking.
The as Keyword
The most common way to assert a type is with the as keyword. You write the expression first, then as TypeName. TypeScript will treat the value as that type from that point on.

blocks.title: Type assertion with as

blocks.text: Angle-Bracket Syntax
TypeScript also supports the older angle-bracket syntax: <TypeName>expression. It is equivalent to as, but cannot be used in .tsx files (JSX) because it conflicts with JSX tags.

blocks.title: Angle-bracket assertion

blocks.text: Non-Null Assertion Operator
The postfix ! operator is a non-null assertion. It tells TypeScript that the value is neither null nor undefined, suppressing the corresponding error.

blocks.title: Non-null assertion

blocks.text: Double Assertions

TypeScript only allows assertions between compatible types. If you need to convert a type to a completely unrelated type (which is usually a sign of a design problem), you can use a double assertion via unknown as an intermediate step.

blocks.title: Double assertion through unknown

blocks.text: Type Guards vs Type Assertions

rows: Yes — narrows based on runtime logic
Only if you are certain of the type

blocks.text: Always prefer type guards over type assertions. An assertion is a promise to the compiler — if you are wrong, you will get a runtime error that TypeScript cannot help you catch. The non-null assertion (!) is especially dangerous; use optional chaining (?.) as a safer alternative where possible.
Type guards allow TypeScript to narrow a broad type to a more specific one inside a conditional block, eliminating runtime errors and enabling full autocomplete on the narrowed type.
The typeof Type Guard
The typeof operator checks primitive types at runtime. TypeScript recognises these checks and narrows the type inside the branch automatically.

blocks.title: typeof narrowing

blocks.text: The instanceof Type Guard
Use instanceof to narrow a union of class types. TypeScript will recognise the guard and give you access to members of the specific class inside that branch.

blocks.title: instanceof narrowing

blocks.text: Custom Type Guard Functions
When built-in operators are insufficient, you can write a user-defined type guard. The return type uses the parameterName is Type predicate syntax to tell TypeScript which type was confirmed.

blocks.title: Custom type predicate

blocks.text: Discriminated Unions & the in Operator

A discriminated union uses a shared literal property (often called a tag or kind) to distinguish union members. The in operator checks whether a property exists on an object and is also recognised as a type guard.

blocks.title: Discriminated union

blocks.text: Add an never exhaustiveness check in the default branch of a switch to get a compile-time error whenever a new union member is added but not handled.

rows: Primitives: string, number, boolean, symbol, bigint
Objects with optional or unique properties

blocks.text: TypeScript ships with a set of built-in generic utility types that transform existing types, removing the need to write repetitive mapped-type boilerplate by hand.
Partial, Required, and Readonly
Partial<T> makes every property of T optional. Required<T> does the opposite, removing all optional modifiers. Readonly<T> prevents any property from being reassigned after construction.

blocks.title: Partial, Required, Readonly

blocks.text: Pick, Omit, and Record
Pick<T, K> constructs a type by selecting a subset of properties K from T. Omit<T, K> excludes those properties instead. Record<K, V> builds an object type mapping keys K to values of type V.

blocks.title: Pick, Omit, Record

blocks.text: Extract, Exclude, and NonNullable
Extract<T, U> keeps only the members of union T that are assignable to U. Exclude<T, U> removes them. NonNullable<T> strips null and undefined from a type.

blocks.title: Extract, Exclude, NonNullable

blocks.text: ReturnType, Parameters, and InstanceType

Function-focused utility types let you derive types from existing functions. ReturnType<T> extracts the return type, Parameters<T> gives a tuple of the parameter types, and InstanceType<T> gets the instance type of a constructor.

blocks.title: ReturnType and Parameters

items: Utility types are just aliases — they do not affect runtime behaviour.
Combine multiple utility types for precise shapes: Readonly<Partial<User>>.
ReturnType is useful when a function's return type isn't explicitly declared.
Record is ideal for dictionary/lookup map patterns.
All utility types are generic and can be composed freely.

blocks.text: Utility types operate at the type level only. They disappear during compilation and produce no JavaScript output.
Mapped types let you create new types by iterating over the keys of an existing type and transforming each property — they are the engine behind most of TypeScript's built-in utility types.
Basic Mapped Type Syntax
The syntax [K in keyof T]: SomeType iterates every key K of type T. You can use T[K] inside the body to preserve the original value type, or replace it with something new.

blocks.title: Custom Readonly mapped type

blocks.text: Modifier Tokens: + and -

Prefix a modifier with - to remove it, or + to add it (the default). For example, -readonly removes the readonly flag, and -? removes the optional modifier — which is exactly how Required<T> is implemented.

blocks.title: Removing optional with -?

blocks.text: Remapping Keys with as

TypeScript 4.1 introduced key remapping via the as clause inside a mapped type. Combined with template literal types you can transform property names at the type level — for example generating getter method names automatically.

blocks.title: Key remapping with template literals

blocks.text: Filtering Keys with never
Returning never from the as clause removes that key entirely from the resulting type, acting as a compile-time filter on properties.

blocks.title: Filtering out non-string values

items: Use [K in keyof T] to iterate over all keys of T.
T[K] preserves the original value type for each key.
The as clause (TypeScript 4.1+) allows key renaming and filtering.
Return never from as to exclude a property from the result.
Mapped types can be combined with conditional types for powerful transformations.
All built-in utility types like Partial, Readonly, and Required are implemented as mapped types.

blocks.text: Mapped types only work on object types. Applying them to primitives like string or number directly produces an empty object type {}.

rows: [K in keyof T as NewKey]: T[K]
[K in keyof T as T[K] extends X ? K : never]: T[K]

blocks.text: Conditional types add if-else logic at the type level, enabling type relationships that depend on other types — unlocking patterns that would otherwise require many manual overloads.
Basic Conditional Type Syntax
The syntax is T extends U ? X : Y. If the type T is assignable to U the result is X, otherwise it is Y. This mirrors a JavaScript ternary but operates entirely at the type level.

blocks.title: Simple conditional type
blocks.code:
type IsString<T> = T extends string ? true : false;

type A = IsString<string>;  // true
type B = IsString<number>;  // false
type C = IsString<'hello'>; // true (string literal extends string)

blocks.text: Distributive Conditional Types

When a conditional type is applied to a naked type parameter (one that is not wrapped), TypeScript distributes the condition over each member of a union automatically. This is how Exclude and Extract work internally.

blocks.title: Distribution over unions
blocks.code:
type Flatten<T> = T extends Array<infer Item> ? Item : T;

type A = Flatten<string[]>;          // string
type B = Flatten<number[]>;          // number
type C = Flatten<string | number[]>; // string | number (distributed)

blocks.text: The infer Keyword

The infer keyword is used inside the extends clause to declare a new type variable that TypeScript infers at the call site. It allows you to extract parts of a type structure — such as the element type of an array, or the return type of a function.

blocks.title: infer to extract types

blocks.text: Practical Conditional Types

Conditional types are especially powerful when building library-level APIs where the return type depends on the input type. The example below shows a type-safe unwrapper that peels one layer of Promise wrapping.

blocks.title: Awaited type (manual implementation)

items: T extends U ? X : Y is the fundamental conditional type syntax.
Conditional types distribute over union types when applied to a naked type parameter.
Use infer to capture and name a type that TypeScript deduces from a pattern.
Wrap T in a tuple [T] extends [U] to prevent distribution when you need non-distributive behaviour.
Recursive conditional types (TypeScript 4.1+) can model deeply nested structures.
The built-in Awaited<T>, ReturnType<T>, and Parameters<T> are all implemented with conditional types and infer.

blocks.text: To disable distribution, wrap the type parameter: [T] extends [U] ? X : Y. This treats the entire type as a single unit rather than distributing over union members.

rows: T extends infer U ? U : never
T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never
T extends [infer H, ...infer T] ? H : never

blocks.text: TypeScript supports both ES module syntax and its own namespace system, giving you fine-grained control over code organisation and encapsulation across files.
ES Modules in TypeScript
TypeScript uses the standard ES module syntax: export to expose declarations and import to consume them. Types and interfaces are erased at compile time and never appear in the emitted JavaScript.

blocks.title: Named exports and imports

blocks.text: Default Exports

A module may have one export default declaration, which can be imported under any name. Default exports are common in frameworks like React and Angular. Prefer named exports for libraries to make tree-shaking easier.

blocks.title: Default export

blocks.text: Re-exporting and Barrel Files

A barrel file (conventionally named index.ts) re-exports from multiple modules to create a clean public API for a directory. Use export { ... } from to re-export named exports or export * from for everything.

blocks.title: Barrel re-export (index.ts)

blocks.text: Namespaces

Namespaces (previously called internal modules) group related declarations under a single name. They are useful for organising global scripts that are not bundled as modules. The namespace keyword compiles to a self-executing function that attaches properties to an object.

blocks.title: Namespace declaration

blocks.text: Prefer ES modules over namespaces for new projects. Namespaces are most useful when writing ambient declaration files or targeting environments without a module bundler.

items: Use import type or export type to import/export types only — they are erased at compile time and help bundlers with tree-shaking.
Dynamic imports import('./module') return a Promise and enable code splitting.
Set moduleResolution: 'bundler' or 'node16' in tsconfig.json to match your bundler's resolution strategy.
Namespaces can span multiple files using reference directives: /// <reference path="..." />
Avoid mixing namespace imports and ES module imports in the same project.

blocks.text: Decorators are a stage-3 JavaScript proposal that TypeScript supports today, providing a declarative way to attach metadata or modify the behaviour of classes, methods, properties, and parameters.
Enabling Decorators

To use decorators, set experimentalDecorators: true in your tsconfig.json. For metadata reflection (used by frameworks like NestJS and Angular), also enable emitDecoratorMetadata: true and install the reflect-metadata package.

blocks.title: tsconfig.json decorator settings

blocks.text: Class Decorators

A class decorator is a function that receives the constructor of the decorated class. It can observe the class, modify it, or return a replacement constructor. The decorator is applied with the @ symbol immediately before the class declaration.

blocks.title: Class decorator

blocks.text: Method Decorators

A method decorator receives the target prototype, the method name, and the property descriptor. It is commonly used to add cross-cutting concerns like logging, timing, or access control without modifying the method body.

blocks.title: Method decorator for logging

blocks.text: Property and Parameter Decorators

A property decorator receives the target and property name. It is most useful for recording metadata. A parameter decorator receives the target, method name, and the parameter index, and is frequently used by dependency injection frameworks to register injectable tokens.

blocks.title: Property decorator

items: Decorators run at class definition time, not at instantiation time.
Multiple decorators on one target are applied bottom-up (closest to the target first).
Class decorators run after all member decorators have been applied.
Decorator factories (functions that return decorators) accept configuration arguments.
NestJS uses decorators heavily: @Controller, @Injectable, @Get, @Body, etc.
Angular uses @Component, @NgModule, @Input, @Output, and @Inject.

blocks.text: TypeScript 5.0 introduced support for the TC39 Stage 3 decorator proposal with different semantics. Check your framework's compatibility before switching from experimentalDecorators to the new standard.
TypeScript's compiler options give you precise control over strictness, output targets, and module resolution — understanding them is essential for maintaining a healthy, scalable codebase.
The tsconfig.json File

All TypeScript compiler settings live in tsconfig.json at the root of your project. The compilerOptions object controls the TypeScript compiler, while include, exclude, and files control which source files are part of the compilation.

blocks.title: Typical tsconfig.json
blocks.code:
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "lib": ["ES2022", "DOM"],
    "strict": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true,
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "rootDir": "./src",
    "declaration": true,
    "sourceMap": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true
  },
  "include": ["src"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist"]
}

blocks.text: The strict Flag

Enabling "strict": true is a single flag that activates a family of individual strict checks. This is the recommended setting for all new projects. You can also enable each sub-flag individually if you need to migrate an existing codebase incrementally.

items: strictNullChecks — null and undefined are not assignable to other types.
strictFunctionTypes — function parameter types are checked contravariantly.
strictBindCallApply — bind, call, and apply are type-checked.
strictPropertyInitialization — class properties must be initialised in the constructor.
noImplicitAny — expressions cannot silently get an implicit any type.
noImplicitThis — this in functions must have an explicit type annotation.
alwaysStrict — emits 'use strict' in every output file.

blocks.text: Additional Strictness Flags

Beyond strict, several additional flags catch more subtle bugs. noUncheckedIndexedAccess adds undefined to the return type of array and index signature accesses. exactOptionalPropertyTypes distinguishes between a missing property and one explicitly set to undefined.

blocks.title: Effect of noUncheckedIndexedAccess

blocks.text: Project References

For large monorepos, TypeScript project references ("references" in tsconfig.json) allow the compiler to build sub-projects independently and cache their outputs. Use tsc --build (or tsc -b) to compile with project references.

blocks.title: Project references setup

blocks.text: Run tsc --noEmit for type-checking without producing output files — ideal for CI pipelines where a bundler (Vite, webpack) handles the actual build.

rows: Adds undefined to index access results
Error if not all code paths return a value

blocks.text: TypeScript ships a comprehensive set of DOM type definitions, letting you write type-safe browser code with full autocomplete for every Web API — from DOM manipulation to Fetch and Web Workers.
Enabling DOM Types
Add "DOM" and "DOM.Iterable" to the lib array in your tsconfig.json. Without this, browser globals like document, window, and HTMLElement will be unknown to the compiler.

blocks.title: tsconfig.json lib setting

blocks.text: Querying the DOM Safely

Methods like document.getElementById return HTMLElement | null. You must check for null before accessing properties. To get a more specific element type, use a generic overload such as document.querySelector<HTMLInputElement>.

blocks.title: Type-safe DOM querying

blocks.text: Typing Event Handlers
Event handlers receive typed event objects. Use MouseEvent, KeyboardEvent, InputEvent, and others to get accurate types for event.target, event.key, and related properties.

blocks.title: Typed event handlers

blocks.text: Fetch API and Typing Responses
The Fetch API is fully typed in TypeScript. The Response.json() method returns Promise<any>, so it is best practice to cast the result to your own interface for type safety.

blocks.title: Type-safe fetch

items: Use non-null assertion (!) only when you are certain an element exists; prefer explicit null checks otherwise.
The as keyword is a type assertion, not a cast — it does not convert values at runtime.
EventTarget and Element are broad base types; use specific subtypes like HTMLInputElement for element-specific properties.
Web Worker global scope uses a different lib: 'WebWorker' instead of 'DOM'.
TypeScript's lib.dom.d.ts is automatically updated with browser APIs as the spec evolves.

blocks.text: Avoid overusing type assertions (as Type). Each assertion is a promise to the compiler that you know better — a wrong assertion will cause a runtime error with no compile-time warning.

Advanced generics unlock reusable abstractions that remain fully type-safe — from constraining type parameters to building higher-order functions that preserve the types of their inputs automatically.
Generic Constraints with extends
Use T extends SomeType in a generic definition to constrain the set of types that can be passed as T. This lets you call properties or methods that are guaranteed to exist on the constrained type.

blocks.title: Generic constraints

blocks.text: Multiple Type Parameters
Generic functions and classes can have multiple type parameters. They can be independent or can reference each other via constraints, enabling precise modelling of relationships between types.

blocks.title: Multiple type parameters

blocks.text: Generic Classes

Classes can be parameterised with type variables, enabling type-safe data structures. The type parameter applies to all methods and properties within the class, ensuring consistent typing throughout its API.

blocks.title: Generic Stack class

blocks.text: Default Type Parameters
Like default function arguments, generic type parameters can have defaults with T = DefaultType. This is useful in component libraries where a type parameter is usually string but can be overridden.

blocks.title: Default type parameters

blocks.text: Variadic Tuple Types
TypeScript 4.0 introduced variadic tuple types, allowing generic rest elements in tuples. Combined with infer, they enable type-safe composition of function argument lists and tuple transformations.

blocks.title: Variadic tuple type

items: Use extends to constrain type parameters and access their members safely.
keyof T produces a union of the string literal key names of T.
T[K] is an indexed access type — it looks up the type of property K on T.
Default type parameters reduce boilerplate for common cases.
Variadic tuple types (TypeScript 4.0+) allow spreading generics into tuples.
Generic interfaces and classes can implement other generic interfaces.

blocks.text: When TypeScript cannot infer a type parameter, it falls back to the constraint (unknown if none is specified). Always provide explicit type arguments when inference would be ambiguous.

rows: K must be a key of T
Type of property K on T

blocks.text: Declaration files describe the types of existing JavaScript code to TypeScript — they are the bridge that gives third-party libraries and plain JS modules full type safety without requiring a rewrite.
What Is a .d.ts File?

A .d.ts file contains only type information and no executable code. TypeScript uses these files for type checking but does not emit them as JavaScript. When you compile TypeScript with "declaration": true, the compiler automatically generates a .d.ts alongside every .js output file.

blocks.title: Generated .d.ts for a module

blocks.text: Ambient Declarations

The declare keyword marks a declaration as ambient — it tells TypeScript "this thing exists at runtime" without providing an implementation. Ambient declarations are the building blocks of hand-written .d.ts files for pure JavaScript libraries.

blocks.title: Ambient declarations

blocks.text: DefinitelyTyped and @types

The DefinitelyTyped repository hosts community-maintained declaration files for thousands of popular JavaScript packages. Install them via npm under the @types scope. TypeScript automatically picks them up from node_modules/@types.

blocks.title: Installing @types packages

blocks.text: Module Augmentation

Module augmentation lets you add new type information to an existing module's declarations — for example, extending Express.Request to include a custom user property added by authentication middleware.

blocks.title: Augmenting Express Request

blocks.text: Global Augmentation
To add properties to the global scope (e.g., extending Window or globalThis), use declare global inside a module file.

blocks.title: Extending the Window interface

blocks.text: A .d.ts file that contains no import or export statements is treated as a script (global scope). Add export {} to make it a module and enable declare global.

items: Set declaration: true in tsconfig.json to auto-generate .d.ts files.
Use skipLibCheck: true to skip type-checking of .d.ts files in node_modules.
The types and typeRoots compiler options control which @types packages are included.
Module augmentation must import the original module to stay in module scope.
Hand-write .d.ts files only for libraries that have no @types package available.
Always ship .d.ts files alongside your npm package if it is written in TypeScript.

blocks.text: TypeScript is the default language in both Angular and is strongly encouraged in React, Vue, and NestJS — each framework provides first-class type definitions that make component development safer and more productive.
TypeScript with React

In React, type your component props with an interface or type alias and pass it as a generic to React.FC or annotate the function directly. Use useState<T>, useRef<T>, and typed event handlers for fully type-safe components.

blocks.title: React functional component with TypeScript

blocks.text: React Hooks with TypeScript
Custom hooks follow the same generic patterns. useReducer benefits greatly from TypeScript — use a discriminated union for actions to get exhaustive checking in the reducer.

blocks.title: useReducer with discriminated union

blocks.text: TypeScript with Angular
Angular is built with TypeScript and uses decorators extensively. Use @Component, @Input(), and @Output() with explicit types. Angular's signal() API (v17+) is fully generic.

blocks.title: Angular standalone component

blocks.text: TypeScript with NestJS

NestJS is an opinionated Node.js framework that leans heavily on TypeScript decorators and dependency injection. DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) are plain classes decorated with validation rules, and the framework infers types from them automatically.

blocks.title: NestJS controller and DTO

items: React: install @types/react and @types/react-dom for type definitions.
Use React.FC<Props> or explicitly type function parameters — both are valid.
Angular projects are TypeScript by default; no additional @types package is needed.
Angular's strictTemplates option in tsconfig catches template type errors at build time.
NestJS uses reflect-metadata and experimentalDecorators; both are pre-configured.
Vue 3 supports TypeScript with defineComponent and the Composition API's <script setup lang='ts'>.

blocks.text: In React, prefer explicit prop interfaces over React.FC for components that accept children — the children prop is no longer implicitly included in React.FC since React 18 types.

rows: Generic hooks, event types, prop interfaces

blocks.text: Migrating a JavaScript codebase to TypeScript does not have to be a big-bang rewrite — TypeScript is designed for incremental adoption, letting you add types file by file while keeping the project functional throughout.
Step 1: Install TypeScript and Initialise tsconfig

Start by installing TypeScript and generating a tsconfig.json. Configure it to allow JavaScript files alongside TypeScript using allowJs: true. This means the compiler processes both .js and .ts files from day one.

blocks.title: Initial tsconfig for migration

blocks.text: Step 2: Rename Files Incrementally

Rename JavaScript files from .js to .ts one at a time, starting with utility files that have no side effects and are imported by many others. Fix type errors file by file. Prioritise files with the most downstream dependents first.

blocks.title: Before and after renaming a utility file

blocks.text: Step 3: Add Types to Function Signatures

Add return types and parameter types to functions first. Use unknown instead of any where the type is genuinely unknown — it forces you to narrow before use. Replace loose Object and {} types with proper interfaces.

blocks.title: Typing an API response handler

blocks.text: Step 4: Enable checkJs and Tighten strictness

Once the majority of files are .ts, enable checkJs: true to type-check any remaining JavaScript files. Then progressively enable individual strict flags — start with strictNullChecks, then noImplicitAny, before finally setting strict: true.

blocks.title: Handling strictNullChecks errors

blocks.text: Using any as a Temporary Escape Hatch

During migration it is acceptable to use any as a temporary measure to unblock compilation. Mark these with a // TODO: fix types comment and track them. Tools like ts-migrate and ESLint's @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any rule help you find and eliminate them systematically.

blocks.title: Temporary any with ESLint suppression

items: Start with allowJs: true so JS and TS files coexist during migration.
Rename files incrementally — leaf modules (no dependencies) first.
Use @ts-check at the top of JS files to get lightweight type checking without renaming.
The ts-migrate CLI tool can auto-annotate files with any to unblock compilation quickly.
Enable strict flags one at a time to manage the volume of errors.
Prioritise typing public APIs (exported functions) over internal implementation details.
Use JSDoc type annotations in remaining .js files as an intermediate step.

blocks.text: Add // @ts-nocheck at the top of a file to silence all TypeScript errors temporarily during migration. Use sparingly and remove it as each file is properly typed.

rows: Convert files to .ts one by one
